ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (mixed dish with meat and rice) used as closest defensible category for a beef yakiniku bibimbap bowl; no exact code exists for this fusion preparation.

main_ingredients: gochujang (sweet and spicy red pepper paste)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g (one bowl), providing around 1228 kcal, 64 g protein, 92 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate per bowl.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Melted Cheese Beef Yakiniku Bibimbap Bowl (G00010192)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010192 with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific fusion dish. GI estimated at 64 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database). The low overall carbohydrate content (8.85 g/100g) and high protein and fat content from beef and cheese will attenuate the postprandial glycemic response. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (64 × 35.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.7, rounded to 22.4 after accounting for fibre adjustment. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of this dish has not been performed.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg/100g — more than double the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; driven by yakiniku sauce, soy sauce, gochujang, kimchi, and cheese
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this dish or request significantly reduced yakiniku sauce, soy sauce, and kimchi portions, as a single 400 g bowl delivers approximately 5116 mg sodium — more than twice the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~64) from Japanese short-grain rice; low reported carbohydrate (8.85 g/100g) but actual bowl carbohydrate load is higher; high protein (16.09 g/100g) supports glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake carefully, as the rice base contributes a meaningful glycemic load per bowl (~22 GL units); request reduced rice portion and reduced gochujang to limit added sugar exposure.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.06 g/100g) from beef and melted cheese, very high sodium (1279 mg/100g), and cholesterol (78 mg/100g); saturated fat content estimated high from cheese and beef combination
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this dish due to its combination of high total fat, high sodium, and cholesterol, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; if consumed, request reduced cheese and sauce portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid), high sodium (1279 mg/100g), gochujang as nightshade ingredient, and estimated pro-inflammatory saturated fat from beef and cheese
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the pro-inflammatory combination of red meat, high sodium, and nightshade-derived gochujang; substituting beef with chicken or tofu yakiniku would reduce arachidonic acid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Melted Cheese Beef Yakiniku Bibimbap Bowl is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish served in Singapore Japanese restaurant chains. It consists of grilled beef prepared in yakiniku style (marinated in a sweet-savory soy-based sauce and grilled), served over 100% Japan-harvested short-grain white rice, topped with shredded and melted cheese, seasoned bean sprouts, seasoned spinach, kimchi (fermented spicy cabbage), gochujang (a sweet and spicy Korean red pepper paste), and a half boiled egg. The dish delivers a rich, umami-forward flavor profile combining the caramelized sweetness of yakiniku beef, the tangy heat of kimchi and gochujang, the creamy saltiness of melted cheese, and the soft texture of Japanese rice. Per 100 g, it provides 307 kcal, 16.09 g protein, 23.06 g fat, 8.85 g carbohydrate, 6.24 g sugar, and 1279 mg sodium. The calcium content is notably high at 1375 mg/100g, attributable to the melted cheese component. Clinically, the dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to extreme sodium (1279 mg/100g) and high fat content. It is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the rice-derived glycemic load. The dish is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to red meat, nightshade ingredients, and high sodium. It is not halal-certified and contains allergens including milk, egg, soy, and gluten.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010192 with confidence 0.95; this is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singapore food records.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source record and is plausible given the low-fibre components (white rice, beef, cheese, egg) dominating the dish, though kimchi and spinach would contribute minor fibre. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo record G00010192 and are not available from the HPB database for this dish as of 2026-04-16. The calcium value of 1375 mg/100g is unusually high and is taken verbatim from the HPB source; this likely reflects the high proportion of melted cheese in the dish formulation. GI is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~64) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement exists for this fusion dish. The dish is classified as Japanese-Korean fusion and is served in Singapore (region_tags: SG); accordingly, the full Singaporean multilingual naming rule is applied: English (food_name), Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases are all provided. Japanese and Korean names are additionally provided given the cuisine fusion components. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.
